visits.note:
S: Patient reports recurrent headaches for the last 6 months, occurring several times a month. Described as a tight band squeezing around the head, worse after long days at work. Denies nausea, vomiting, photophobia, or phonophobia.
O: BP 120/80, Heart Rate 70. Tenderness in neck and shoulder muscles.
A: For physician review: Suggestive of recurrent tension headache (ICD-10 suggestion: G44.2 — for physician confirmation only).
P: Stress management and regular sleep. Ibuprofen 400mg as needed for acute episodes (limit to 2-3 days per week).

S: Patient returned for follow up on tension headaches. Reports 50% reduction in headache frequency. O: BP 122/78, HR 72. A: Suggestive of improving recurrent tension headache (G44.2). P: Continue stress management and PRN ibuprofen.

S: Patient reports 50% improvement in headache frequency.
O: BP 122/78.
A: Suggestive of improving tension headache (G44.2).
P: Continue stress management.

⚠️ URGENT ESCALATION REQUIRED

S: Patient reports 50% improvement in headache frequency. O: BP 122/78. A: Suggestive of improving tension headache (G44.2). P: Continue stress management.

**S – Subjective**
* Chief Complaint: Cough for about a week
* Symptoms & History: The patient reports a cough that started dry but has been productive of yellowish mucus for the last three days, fever of 101°F for the first two days, shortness of breath when climbing stairs, sore throat at the beginning, and body aches. The patient has been taking paracetamol and an over-the-counter cough syrup.
* Medications & Social History: The patient does not smoke, has no history of asthma, COPD, or allergies, and has been in contact with a family member who had a cold recently.
**O – Objective**
* Vital Signs: Oxygen saturation is 97%, heart rate is 92 beats per minute, blood pressure is 122 over 78, and temperature is 98.6°F.
* Physical Exam & Lab Findings: Crackles heard in the right lower lung.
**A – Assessment**
* For physician review: Possible mild bacterial chest infection, early community-acquired pneumonia, or acute bronchitis.
**P – Plan**
* Next Steps & Interventions: The patient has been prescribed amoxicillin-clavulanate for five days, advised to stay hydrated, and to use paracetamol if fever returns. A chest X-ray and complete blood count have been ordered.
* Follow-Up & Recommendations: Follow-up in five days or sooner if symptoms worsen.

S: Patient presents for hypertension follow-up. Reports feeling well on Lisinopril 10mg without side effects. Home BP checks run in the 130s/80s.
O: BP 132/82, Heart Rate 68. Lungs clear to auscultation. Renal function and electrolytes normal.
A: For physician review: Essential hypertension, stable and well-controlled (ICD-10 suggestion: I10 — for physician confirmation only).
P: Continue Lisinopril 10mg daily. Follow-up and basic metabolic panel (BMP) in 3 months.

S: Patient reports mild dizziness on standing. No syncope or chest pain. O: BP 118/72 (orthostatic drop from 130/80). Heart Rate 76. A: For physician review: Possible orthostatic hypotension (ICD-10 suggestion: I95.1). P: Increase fluid intake, advise slow position changes. Follow up in 2 weeks.

**S – Subjective**
* Chief Complaint: Headache for 3 days, worse in the morning
* Symptoms & History: Patient reports headache for 3 days, worsening in the morning, without nausea
* Medications & Social History: Patient is on Lisinopril 10mg daily for hypertension

**O – Objective**
* Vital Signs: BP 130/85
* Physical Exam & Lab Findings: No notable physical exam or lab findings reported

**A – Assessment**
* For physician review: Possible tension-type headache (ICD-10 suggestions: G44.229, G44.219, or R51.9 for unspecified headache)

**P – Plan**
* Next Steps & Interventions: Consider over-the-counter pain relief options, advise patient to maintain a headache diary to track frequency and severity
* Follow-Up & Recommendations: Schedule follow-up appointment in 1-2 weeks to reassess headache symptoms and adjust plan as needed

S: Patient reports productive cough with yellow sputum for 5 days, mild sore throat, and subjective fever. Denies shortness of breath, chest pain, or wheezing.
O: Temperature 99.1 F, Heart Rate 80, Respiratory Rate 16, O2 Saturation 98% on room air. Mild scattered wheezing, good air entry. Erythematous throat without exudate.
A: For physician review: Suspected acute bronchitis (ICD-10 suggestion: J20.9 — for physician confirmation only) or upper respiratory infection.
P: Drink warm fluids, honey for cough, OTC guaifenesin as needed. Return to clinic if symptoms worsen, high fever, or shortness of breath develops.

S: Patient reports rolling right ankle outward during basketball yesterday. Developed immediate swelling and pain on weight bearing.
O: Right ankle shows swelling and tenderness over the anterior talofibular ligament. No tenderness over medial malleolus or base of the fifth metatarsal. BP 118/76, Pulse 75.
A: For physician review: Suggested right ankle sprain (ICD-10 suggestion: S93.401A — for physician confirmation only).
P: RICE protocol (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation). OTC ibuprofen for pain, limit weight bearing, use ankle brace. Follow up in 1 week if no improvement.

**S – Subjective**
* Chief Complaint: Cough for about a week, not improving
* Symptoms & History: The patient reports a cough that started dry but has been productive of yellowish mucus for the last three days. Initial fever of 101°F for two days, now resolved. Patient experiences shortness of breath when climbing stairs but no chest pain. Sore throat at the beginning, now mostly resolved. No runny nose. Patient has been feeling tired all week and had body aches when the fever started.
* Medications & Social History: The patient has been taking paracetamol for fever and an over-the-counter cough syrup. No smoking history. No history of asthma, COPD, or allergies. Recent contact with a family member who had a cold.

**O – Objective**
* Vital Signs: Oxygen saturation 97%, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, temperature 98.6°F
* Physical Exam & Lab Findings: Crackles heard in the right lower lung. Pending chest X-ray and complete blood count results.

**A – Assessment**
* For physician review: The patient's presentation could suggest a mild bacterial chest infection, possibly early community-acquired pneumonia or acute bronchitis, given the symptoms and physical exam findings. Differential diagnoses may include other respiratory infections. ICD-10 code suggestions based on the retrieved context include J20.9 for acute bronchitis, unspecified.

**P – Plan**
* Next Steps & Interventions: The patient has been prescribed amoxicillin-clavulanate for five days. Continue hydration and use paracetamol if fever returns. The patient is to return in five days for follow-up or sooner if symptoms worsen.
* Follow-Up & Recommendations: Patient education on the importance of completing the antibiotic course, monitoring for worsening symptoms, and when to seek immediate medical attention.

⚠️ URGENT ESCALATION REQUIRED
Subjective
* Chief Complaint: Patient reports severe chest pain radiating to the left arm, accompanied by excessive sweating.
* Symptoms & History: The patient states the onset of symptoms occurred approximately 30 minutes ago, with associated shortness of breath.
* Medications & Social History: No information provided in the transcript.

Objective
* Vital Signs: Not explicitly stated, but the patient is experiencing severe chest pain and shortness of breath.
* Physical Exam & Lab Findings: ECG to be performed immediately as part of emergency response.

Assessment
* Differential possibilities: For physician review, pending confirmation. The symptoms suggest a potential myocardial infarction (heart attack), but clinical judgment and further evaluation are required for a definitive diagnosis.

Plan
* Next Steps & Interventions: The emergency team has been activated. The patient is to receive aspirin and oxygen immediately, with an ECG to be performed urgently.
* Follow-Up & Recommendations: This case requires immediate in-person emergency evaluation. Do not proceed with routine documentation alone.
